titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to his mother, Clara Mary Davidson, 28 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme. James was born in 1877.James writes that he expects to be moving from holding the line in the next day or two to undergo strenuous training, indicating that he would not have time to write as much. He also writes that he had recently been spent a day at the French machine gun school. He is sending clothing home and asks to have other items mailed to him.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
28/5/16 My dearest Mother, I have not been treating
you well recently in the
way of letters but really
it has been impossible
to find time for writing.
Every day it is in the same
from early morning till
late at night, one continual
rush. I was so
sorry dear Mother that
you have had so much
worry with cooks etc
I do wish you could
find someone who would
suit. Was glad to get
a letter also from Father
with news of the pistol.
If I have time I will
write him a line separately. We are probably moving
back from the Line toâ
morrow or day after.
You see we dont get
much notice, for even
yet it is not definite.
We only go a short
distance &, as I understand,
for a short time, in order
to go through some
special training of a
rather strenuous nature,
so it wont mean much
rest. However it will
be a little relief from
the responsibility of
holding the Line. The weather has not
been quite so hot last
few days & there has
been some rain which
was really badly needed
to lay the dust! The
Germans too have been
less vicious so things
have been more or less
peaceful & it has given
us a good opportunity
to push on with a lot of
rather difficult work. I spent a most interesting
day this week at the
French Machine Gun School
some miles from here.
Went with 2 other officers
in a Div. motor & got
back same day. Saw
no end of interesting
things & the French
officers took no end
of trouble to show &
explain everything. Have sent off a parcel
of warm underclothing
today & hope it will l
turn up all right.
Will have some more
to send later. By the
way the lining you
sent for my waterproof
was not the right one.
It belonged to one of
my coats at home.
The correct one is very
similar & you could
easily make a mistake
with them. You can
recognise the one I
want as there are
elastic bands at
wrists & it is rather
shorter than the one
you sent. Would you
please have the other
sent off & then when
it arrives I will send
back the one I have.
The parcels from Fureys
& Malcolmsons don't
arrive so regularly as
they used too but I am
sure it is entirely the
fault of the P.O. Now dearest Mother
goodbye. Don't be
alarmed if you do not
hear from me for some
days as if we do move
it will mean a busy
time for me. Hoping
you are all well & with
fondest love
Your loving son Jim
On Active Service
written 28th May â 2nd June
19/16 Mrs. S. C. Davidson Seacourt Bangor Co. Down
JsDavidson 28 May 1916
